---
title: Session 04 - XXX
number-sections: false
description: XXX
---

# Overview

| Topic                             | Duration | Notes                  |
| :-------------------------------- | :------: | ---------------------- |
| Wrap-up Homework 1                |    30    | Ch. Experimentology    |
| Planning the design               |   120    | with 25 min group task |


: {.striped}




## Homework 2 (individually)

Please read "[The preregistration revolution](https://www.pnas.org/content/115/11/2600)" by Nosek et al. (2018). Answer the following questions succintly in Moodle. As always, you can answer in German or English.

- Q1: Give a short definition of "prediction" and "postdiction".
- Q2: What is the problem when pre- and postdiction are not properly distinguished?
- Q3: What is the hindsight bias?
- Q4: Researchers often deviate from their preregistration, losing some of its benefits. Even in case of a major deviation: What advantage does a preregistration still give?
- Q5: Read the challenges and think about which challenge might apply to our own research design. Select one of the challenges, and describe shortly how this applies to our research project, and what might be done about it.

<!-- ANSWERS:

Q1: 
Prediction: Forming a hypothesis before seeing the data and then collecting data to test the hypothesis. 
Postdiction: Try to explain why something has happened after seeing the data.

Q2:
You find evidence for effects which do not really exist, since you can find a post-hoc explanation to everything.
"Results” are easier to publish since you always “have the claim to support the data”.

Q3:
Thinking that some outcomes are really predictable after actually seeing them happen, even if they were not that predictable.

Q4:
You can still report and explain changes you make on your initial plan. It is then possible to analyze the impact of those changes, this is very good for the internal validity of the study.

Q5:

 -->
